entry.title: Re: corsfilter on /medias
entry.content:
Dear @phoude ,

The /medias endpoint is handled by the mediaweb extension (web application), which has its own separate CORS filter configuration. Without explicit CORS properties for mediaweb, no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is returned for media requests, causing the browser to block them.

we request to maintain separate cors filter for medias further and do test in local or lower environment first.

corsfilter.mediaweb.

 

Regards,

Prasanth

SAP Support

entry.title: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content:
As application landscapes grow and user adoption increases, performance issues in custom CDS (Core Data Services) views can emerge in ways that are not immediately obvious.
Two patterns in particular are responsible for a significant share of unexpected CPU spikes in production HANA systems:

  1. CDS views using locale-based text retrieval that silently bypass HANA's most efficient execution engine.
  2. ABAP programs passing large filter lists to CDS views, generating thousands of SQL filter conditions at runtime.

Both patterns are common in custom development, both are easy to miss during development and testing, and both have clean, well-documented solutions. This post walks through each one in detail.

The technical foundation of this post is based on knowledge shared by @jayaprakash_subramanian .
His willingness to share his expertise on HANA execution internals and CDS optimization made this post possible - thank you.

Contents

  1. Step 0 — Diagnose Before You Fix
  2. Fix #1 — CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine
  3. Fix #2 — Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables
  4. General Best Practices
  5. Summary
  6. References

Step 0: Before You Fix — Diagnose First

Before applying any optimization, confirm which queries are causing elevated CPU usage.
The SQL Plan Cache in SAP HANA is the primary tool for this.

Check the Execution Engine of a Specific Query

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH, STATEMENT_STRING
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column reveals which engine processed the query:

ValueMeaning
HEXHigh-Performance Analytical Engine — optimal for CDS views
COLUMNColumn Engine — less optimized, higher CPU for CDS workloads
ROWRow Engine — used for row-store tables, not typical for CDS views

If you see COLUMN where you expect HEX, the query is not running at its full potential.

Monitor CPU and Memory at the System Level

SAP HANA Cockpit and HANA Studio both provide dashboards for CPU and memory usage over configurable time windows.
Key indicators to watch:

  • CPU usage spikes above 70–80% during business hours, correlated with Fiori app usage.
  • Out-of-Memory (OOM) dumps in HANA trace files or HANA Cockpit - these indicate memory pressure from specific statements.
  • Statement Memory Limit - if individual SQL statements approach the configured limit, they may be terminated or cause cascading failures.
Tip: Always capture both a 7-day and a 24-hour view to distinguish between sustained load and isolated spikes.
This distinction determines whether a fix is urgent or can be planned during a maintenance window.

CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine

SAP Note 3513507  ·  SAP Note 3076534

Background: How SAP HANA Handles Multilingual Text

SAP systems are designed to operate in multiple languages.
Many business entities - such as product descriptions, unit of measure texts, and customer category labels - are stored in dedicated text tables: separate database tables containing one row per language per entity.


Entity KeyLanguage KeyDescription
ITEM-001ENSteel Pipe
ITEM-001DEStahlrohr
ITEM-001HEצינור פלדה
ITEM-001JA鋼管

When a user opens a Fiori application with their session language set to Hebrew (he), the system must return the Hebrew text row and render it correctly in right-to-left script.
The same mechanism applies to any language that differs from the system installation default.

What WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) Does

In SAP HANA CDS, text tables are modeled using the @ObjectModel.text.association annotation.
When a Fiori application reads a CDS view backed by such an association, it automatically appends a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause to the generated SQL query, passing the current user's session locale as the parameter value.

SELECT *
FROM MY_CDS_VIEW
WITH PARAMETERS ('LOCALE' = 'he')

This instructs HANA to join the text table and return only the rows matching the Hebrew language key.
Without this parameter, the CDS view would either return texts for all languages or fall back to a system default, neither of which is appropriate for a user-facing Fiori application.

Example — Hebrew locale in action

A product or entity search CDS view with LOCALE = 'he' returns Hebrew descriptions:

KeyDescription
ITEM-001צינור פלדה (Steel Pipe)
ITEM-002ספק ראשי בע"מ (Main Supplier Ltd.)
ITEM-003חברת דוגמה בע"מ (Example Company Ltd.)

The Problem: HEX Engine Limitation

SAP HANA's HEX (High-Performance Analytical Execution) Engine is the modern columnar execution engine optimized for CDS views accessed from Fiori applications.
It delivers substantially lower CPU consumption compared to the fallback Column Engine, particularly for queries involving joins, projections, and filters typical of CDS-based reads.

However, as documented in SAP Note 3513507, the HEX engine has a known limitation: it cannot process queries containing a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause.
When such a query arrives, HANA silently falls back to the Column Engine.

Why this goes undetected: This fallback is entirely invisible during development and unit testing.
Results are correct - just processed less efficiently.
The impact only becomes apparent in production, where many users simultaneously access the same Fiori apps in a non-English locale.

This is particularly relevant in any deployment where the working language differs from the system installation default - including Hebrew, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Thai, and others.

The Solution: Adding the HEX Hint

There are two approaches, and the correct choice depends on whether you own the CDS view or not.

Option 1 — Preferred: Add the Hint Directly in the CDS View

If the CDS view is a custom view that you own and can modify, the preferred approach is to embed the DB hint directly in the CDS view definition using a consumption annotation. This is the clean, transport-safe, source-controlled solution described in SAP Note 2717265.
In the CDS view DDL, add the hint annotation:
@DatabaseHint: [{dbSystem: #HDB, hint: 'USE_HEX_PLAN'}]
define view entity YOUR_CDS_VIEW ...​
Or in the ABAP SELECT consuming the view, use the %_HINTS addition:
 
SELECT *
  FROM your_cds_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  %_HINTS HDB 'USE_HEX_PLAN'.
This approach keeps the optimization explicit, versioned, and visible in the development layer.

Option 2 — Fallback: ANNOTATION Hint at the HANA Layer

If the CDS view is a standard SAP-delivered view that you cannot modify, or if you need a temporary fix while a proper code change is being prepared, you can apply the hint at the HANA database layer using the ANNOTATION mechanism (SAP Note 3076534).
 
Important: This should not be the first choice. It is a workaround - applied at the database layer outside the ABAP development lifecycle.
Use it only when Option 1 is not applicable.
 
-- Apply the hint
ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" SET 'HINT' = 'USE_HEX_PLAN';

-- Confirm the annotation is registered
SELECT * FROM ANNOTATIONS;

-- Clear the SQL Plan Cache so HANA generates a fresh execution plan
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

To roll back:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" UNSET 'HINT';

Regardless of which option is used, verify the result the same way:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column should now show HEX.

Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables

SAP Note 2399993 -  SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

The Problem: How ABAP Generates Thousands of SQL Filter Conditions

A common ABAP pattern for reading data from a CDS view is to build a range table and pass it using the IN operator:

SELECT *
FROM zcds_my_view
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
WHERE key_field IN @LR_range.

This is clean, readable ABAP - but it has a critical hidden cost at the database layer.
When ABAP's DB interface translates this statement into SQL, it expands the range table into a series of explicit filter conditions:

SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MY_VIEW
WHERE KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE001'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE002'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE003'
   -- ... potentially thousands more conditions

If lr_range contains hundreds or thousands of entries - entirely realistic for document processing, purchase order workflows, or batch jobs - HANA must parse and compile this statement each time it is executed.
This compilation overhead, not the data retrieval itself, is what drives CPU spikes and degraded response times.

Why this goes undetected: In development and testing, range tables are typically small (a handful of test records).
The problem scales silently with production data volumes, only surfacing once the system is under real load.

This pattern is additionally harmful because:

  • It generates near-identical but technically distinct SQL statements (different values each run), preventing effective plan cache reuse.
  • It worsens over time as data volumes and document counts grow.

The Solution: FOR ALL ENTRIES with HANA FDA Hint

Replace the IN operator with FOR ALL ENTRIES IN, combined with a HANA Fast Data Access (FDA) hint:

SELECT *
  FROM zcds_my_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @LR_range
  WHERE key_field = @LR_range-low
  %_HINTS HDB '&prefer_join_with_fda 1&'.

Why This Works: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

FDA is a SAP HANA optimization specifically designed for FOR ALL ENTRIES queries. Instead of expanding the input list into SQL filter conditions, HANA:

  1. Loads the input list into a HANA-managed temporary table in memory.
  2. Joins the CDS view result against this temporary table.
  3. Lets the HANA query optimizer determine the most efficient join strategy.

The result is a single, stable, reusable SQL execution plan regardless of how many entries are in the input list.
Compilation overhead is eliminated, plan cache reuse is maximized, and CPU consumption is substantially reduced.

Comparison: IN vs FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA

AspectIN '@LR_range'FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA
SQL generatedOne condition per range entrySingle join against temp table
Compile timeGrows with range sizeConstant
Plan cache reuseLow (unique SQL per execution)High (stable plan)
CPU impact at scaleHighLow
ABAP complexityMinimalSlightly higher

 

Important considerations for FOR ALL ENTRIES: If the input table is empty, FOR ALL ENTRIES returns no rows, unlike IN which would return all rows in that case.
It also implicitly removes duplicate rows from the result set.
Ensure your application logic accounts for both behaviors before applying this change.
 

General Best Practices

Always Test in a Non-Production System First

Both fixes involve changes at the database or ABAP layer that affect execution plans.
Apply in a development or quality system first, execute the affected Fiori apps or programs, verify correct results, and confirm the execution plan has changed as expected via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE before promoting to production.

Manage the SQL Plan Cache Proactively

After applying any execution hint or changing ABAP logic, clear the relevant SQL Plan Cache entries.
Leaving stale entries in place will delay the effect of any optimization until the cache entry naturally expires.

-- Clear cache entries for a specific CDS view
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

-- Clear cache entries for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_HASH = 'your_statement_hash_here';

Monitor Regularly, Not Just Reactively

Set up routine monitoring for:

  • CPU usage trends over 7-day rolling windows - distinguishes growing load from isolated incidents.
  • Top SQL statements by CPU time via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE or HANA Cockpit's SQL Monitor.
  • Out-of-Memory dumps - a leading indicator of memory pressure before they become business-impacting outages.

Early detection allows you to address performance issues during planned maintenance windows rather than under incident pressure.

Summary

Both issues share a common theme: code that looks correct and performs acceptably in development causes measurable production impact at scale.
The fixes are targeted, low-risk, and reversible.

IssueRoot CauseSolutionSAP Note
High CPU on CDS views with localeWITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) prevents HEX engine usageApply USE_HEX_PLAN hint via ANNOTATION

3513507, 3076534,
2717265

High CPU from large filter listsABAP IN operator generates thousands of SQL conditionsUse FOR ALL ENTRIES with FDA hint2399993

 

References

  • SAP Note 3513507 - HANA HEX Engine not used for queries containing WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)
  • SAP Note 3076534 - Temporary use of ANNOTATION HINT
  • SAP Note 2399993 - FAQ: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)
  • SAP Note 2717265 - ABAP CDS: Using Database Hints via Annotations (preferred approach for custom CDS views)
  • SAP Help Portal — Accessing Localized Texts in CDS Views
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — SQL Plan Cache

entry.title: Re: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance in S/4HANA: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content: The HEX fallback issue highlights a deeper challenge in SAP HANA abstraction layers. Developers assume CDS equals optimized execution, but hidden clauses like locale parameters silently change the execution path. This reinforces the need for plan cache validation as a standard development discipline, not a reactive troubleshooting step.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Operations: The Complete Live Demo Series 2026
entry.content: The structure of this series is interesting because it mirrors real operational pain points: integrations, jobs, users, and configurations. Instead of focusing on features, it reflects how operations teams actually experience system health across multiple dimensions in day-to-day SAP environments.

entry.title: Re: stock removal not happening while doing MIGO because...
entry.content: It looks like the barch activation caused stock inconsistencies. How did you active batch management precisely?

entry.title: Re: Is SAP CPE the Missing Answer to Geopolitical Raw Material Price Volatility?
entry.content: Thank you so much @MethaqSaif .

entry.title: Re: ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code
entry.content: The most important part here is not just the tooling, but the workflow simplification. Being able to create, activate, and manage ABAP objects directly from VS Code reduces context switching significantly, which is often one of the biggest productivity drains in enterprise development.

entry.title: Re: stock removal not happening while doing MIGO because...
entry.content: Existing stock without batch cannot be consumed once batch management is active. You must clear the old non-batch stock and repost it with batch information. Going forward, always activate batch management before the first goods receipt to avoid mismatches.

entry.title: No Event Mesh? No Problem — Integrating Service Orders to FSM via CPI Polling
entry.content:
SAP Field Service Management (FSM) relies on timely and accurate service order data from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to dispatch technicians, schedule work, and close jobs efficiently. The standard integration between these two systems leverages SAP Event Mesh — a powerful, cloud-native messaging backbone on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — to stream real-time service order lifecycle events into FSM.

However, not every customer has an active SAP Event Mesh subscription on BTP. This could be due to licensing constraints, cost considerations, or an IT strategy that minimises the number of BTP service subscriptions. For these customers, the standard event-driven integration path hits a wall before it even starts.

In this blog, I will walk through a practical workaround: a custom SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) polling iFlow that periodically queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API, formats the result as a synthetic Event Mesh payload, and hands it off to the standard "Replicate Service Order from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP FSM" iFlow — no Event Mesh subscription required.

Note: This work will have some limitations and considersations before adopting.

How the Standard Integration Works

The standard SAP-delivered integration uses communication arrangement SAP_COM_0092 on the S/4HANA Cloud side to publish business events to SAP Event Mesh. A subscriber iFlow on CPI then picks up those events and replicates the affected service orders into FSM.

The four events subscribed to in the standard scenario are:

  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Released/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Completed/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ReleaseRevoked/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ChgdWhenReleased/v1

Each event carries a minimal payload (service order ID, UUID, type, and description). The CPI iFlow then fetches the full service order details and pushes them into FSM. This architecture is clean, near-real-time, and fully supported by SAP — but it has one hard prerequisite: SAP Event Mesh must be provisioned and configured on BTP.

The Challenge: Event Mesh as a Hard Prerequisite

For customers who have already established an S/4HANA Cloud-to-FSM integration but do not have an Event Mesh subscription, the above architecture creates a blocking dependency. Specifically:

  1. SAP Event Mesh is a paid, separately subscribed BTP service. Customers who have not planned for it may not have it available.
  2. Activating Event Mesh mid-project introduces procurement, provisioning, and configuration lead time — often weeks — that can delay go-live.
  3. Some customers deliberately keep their BTP footprint minimal. Adding Event Mesh solely for service order integration conflicts with their platform governance model.
  4. In brownfield or upgrade scenarios, re-configuring communication arrangements and queue subscriptions can introduce regression risk to existing integrations.

These constraints call for an alternative approach — one that achieves the same functional outcome (service order replication into FSM) without any dependency on SAP Event Mesh.

Workaround approach: CPI Polling iFlow

The workaround introduces a lightweight custom polling iFlow in CPI that mimics the role of SAP Event Mesh — without actually using it. The iFlow runs on a scheduled timer (for example, every 10 minutes), queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API for records changed since the last run, constructs a synthetic Event Mesh-style JSON payload for each changed order, and invokes the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow via HTTPS — exactly as if the message had arrived from Event Mesh.

Crucially, the standard iFlow is left completely untouched. This ensures SAP support scope is preserved and future upgrades to the standard content remain non-breaking.

Layer

Component

Remark

Source

S/4HANA Cloud – API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV OData API

Standard API, no change needed

Custom iFlow

CPI Polling iFlow (customer-built)

Scheduled every ~10 min

Payload Builder

Constructs synthetic EMS JSON message

Mimics Event Mesh envelope

Target

Standard CPI iFlow – Replicate Service Order

Called via HTTPS, unmodified

Destination

SAP FSM

Unchanged end target

 

Step 1: Querying Changed Service Orders

The polling iFlow calls the standard OData API below, substituting the timestamp from the last successful run (stored as a CPI local variable):

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV/A_ServiceOrder
  ?$select=ServiceOrder,ServiceObjectType,ServiceOrderUUID,ServiceOrderDescription,ServiceOrderType,ServiceDocChangedDateTime,ServiceOrderIsReleased,ServiceOrderIsCompleted,ServiceOrderIsRejected
  &$filter=(
    ServiceDocChangedDateTime gt datetimeoffset'<LAST_RUN_TIMESTAMP>'
    and (ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO2'
         or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO2')
    and (ServiceOrderIsReleased eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsCompleted eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsRejected eq 'X')
  )

The filter targets only the service order types that are relevant for FSM replication (SVO1, SVO2, RPO1, RPO2) and only records that are in a terminal or active business state (Released, Completed, or Rejected). This keeps the result set lean and avoids processing draft or in-progress orders that FSM does not yet need.

The last-run timestamp is read from a CPI local variable at the start of each execution and updated to the current timestamp at the end of a successful run. This creates a sliding window of changes and avoids re-processing already-replicated orders.

Step 2: Splitting and Iterating

The OData response may return multiple service orders in a single call. The iFlow uses a Splitter step to process each service order independently. This is important because:

  • A failure for one service order does not block replication of others.
  • Error handling and retry logic can be applied at the individual record level.
  • The standard iFlow expects a single service order event per invocation, matching the one-event-per-message model of SAP Event Mesh.

Step 3: Constructing the Synthetic Event Mesh Payload

For each service order, the iFlow builds a JSON payload that is structurally identical to what the standard iFlow would receive from SAP Event Mesh. The event type in the payload is determined by the status flags returned by the OData API:

  • ServiceOrderIsReleased = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Released.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsCompleted = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Completed.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsRejected = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.ReleaseRevoked.v1

A sample constructed payload looks like this:

{
  "type": "sap.s4.beh.serviceorder.v1.ServiceOrder.Released.v1",
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "source": "/default/sap.s4.beh/0LEUVB6",
  "id": "42010aef-4c3b-1fe1-8eaa-82de726150ac",
  "time": "2026-04-16T05:21:15Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "ServiceOrder": "8000000176",
    "CustMgmtObjectType": "BUS2000116",
    "ServiceOrderUUID": "42010aef-4c8d-1fd1-8de5-b5128633bb6f",
    "ServiceOrderDescription": "Annual maintenance visit",
    "ServiceOrderType": "SVO1"
  }
}

The id field can be populated using the ServiceOrderUUID, and the time field is set to the current execution timestamp. The source field should match the BTP source identifier configured in the target standard iFlow — check the iFlow's configuration parameters to confirm the correct value.

Step 4: Invoking the Standard iFlow via HTTPS

Rather than using a ProcessDirect channel (which would require modifying the standard iFlow to expose an internal endpoint), the custom iFlow calls the standard iFlow's public HTTPS endpoint. The technical user performing this call must be assigned the WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy role in CPI to have permission to trigger iFlow executions.

This design keeps a clean boundary: the standard iFlow remains a black box from the custom iFlow's perspective. If SAP updates the standard content in a future release, no changes are needed in the custom polling iFlow as long as the input message contract (the EMS JSON structure) remains stable.

Here is a crisp, highly structured version of your content, optimized for quick scanning and professional review.

⚠️ Limitations & Considerations: Polling Workaround

While this polling workaround is a pragmatic alternative, it lacks the native capabilities of an event-driven architecture. Review these 7 critical limitations before deploying to production.

1. Polling Latency (Near-Real-Time, Not Real-Time)

  • The Gap: Scheduled runs (e.g., every 10 mins) introduce a maximum data lag equal to the polling interval, whereas an event-driven model pushes changes in seconds.

  • Impact: Field techs may act on stale, modified, or canceled service order data on their mobile apps—a high risk for emergency work orders.

  • Mitigation: Shorten the polling interval during peak hours, balancing against S/4HANA Cloud API rate limits.

2. Snapshot-Only (No Intermediate States)

  • The Gap: Polling only captures a snapshot of the latest state. If an order quickly transitions through multiple statuses between runs (e.g., Released $\rightarrow$ Modified $\rightarrow$ Completed), intermediate states are lost.

  • Impact: FSM business rules triggered by specific state transitions (like ChgdWhenReleased or parts checks) will be skipped, causing workflow inconsistencies.

  • Mitigation: Redesign FSM business rules (if any) to trigger actions based on the final current state rather than historical transitions.

3. Missing ReleaseRevoked Context

  • The Gap: The standard integration publishes a distinct ReleaseRevoked event when a released order is recalled. Polling only detects a raw "Rejected/Revoked" status and cannot determine the historical sequence.

  • Impact: SLA timers or automations meant to cancel upon a revoke action may fail to trigger if the release and revoke both happen within a single polling window.

4. Increased S/4HANA Cloud API Load

  • The Gap: Unlike push events, polling repeatedly queries S/4HANA OData APIs on a fixed schedule, even when no data has changed.

  • Impact: Unnecessary network traffic and rapid consumption of your S/4HANA API license quotas.

  • Mitigation: Implement an adaptive schedule (e.g., poll less frequently overnight/weekends) and routinely monitor API usage logs.

5. Loss of ChgdWhenReleased Granularity

  • The Gap: The standard event model sends precise, field-level delta triggers (e.g., only the schedule time changed). Polling can only see that a change occurred, forcing a full-record refresh to FSM.

  • Impact: FSM automations that depend on specific field triggers (like sending an SMS to a tech only if the priority escalates) cannot be targeted cleanly. FSM will only see a bulk data overwrite.

6. Timestamp-Based Edge Cases

  • The Gap: Delta detection relies strictly on filtering the ServiceDocChangedDateTime field.

  • Impact: Clock drift between CPI and S/4HANA can cause skipped or double-processed records.

    If the CPI local variable storing the Last Run Timestamp is wiped (via redeployment or restart), data will be missed without a manual reset. Boundary records matching the exact timestamp filter might be dropped.

  • Mitigation: Build a 1–2 minute overlap window into your timestamp logic, and set up a monitoring alert for missing or stale timestamp variables.

Conclusion

The absence of SAP Event Mesh is not a roadblock for integrating S/4HANA Cloud service orders with SAP FSM. A custom CPI polling iFlow that mimics an Event Mesh payload offers a viable, non-invasive workaround. By keeping the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow completely unmodified, this conservative approach isolates the custom logic into a single, easily maintainable layer.

However, teams must accept the trade-offs: near-real-time latency, the loss of intermediate state history, and added operational complexity around error handling and timestamp management. With a robust retry mechanism and proper monitoring, this architecture can reliably support production workloads.

entry.title: Maintain another exchange rate type different from type M for the Monitor Purchase Order Items app
entry.content:
I've tried to maintain another exchange rate type to display data for Monitor Purchase Order Items app. I found SAP Note
--- Steps to Reproduce ---

App: Monitor Purchase Order Items


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Maintain another exchange rate type different from ty...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you for your patience while I looked into your question.

I can confirm that the behavior you observed is standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

As outlined in SAP Note 3681306, the Monitor Purchase Order Items app uses exchange rate type “M” for currency conversion in purchasing analytics. The exchange rate is derived from the maintained currency exchange rates based on the Purchase Order date. Even if a different or fixed exchange rate is maintained at the purchase order level, it is not considered by this analytical app.

At present, there is no configuration available to change the exchange rate type used in this app. Therefore, the system will continue to use exchange rate type M by design.

If your business requires reporting based on a different exchange rate type,you may consider submitting an enhancement request via the SAP Customer Influence program for Product Management review in future releases.

I hope this helps clarify the behavior. Please feel free to reach out if you have any further questions—I’ll be happy to support you further.

Best Regards

Aishwarya

entry.title: Re: Options blocked in app Define Location: Change
entry.content:
Good day @S0027295288 

Thank you for your question.

That block you are experiencing is most likely expected behaviour because, in configuration activities, deletion is controlled per SSCUI and it is not determined only by user roles. A user can have full authorisation to change an entry, but deletion can still be blocked if SAP does not support deletion for that configuration activity.

Here are references for more guidance

3381770 - Delete entries from configuration activity (SSCUI) - SAP for Me

3418591 - Delete functional area in SSCUI 102363 Define Functional Area -SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3668574 - Deletion is not supported in SSCUI 101326 "Define Business Places for Goods and Services Tax for India" in S/4HANA Public Cloud - SAP for Me

I recommend you do the following:

  1. Confirm the exact SSCUI, which is like 102783. See KBA 3442731 for more details.
  2. Check whether the user in question can change the entry but cannot delete it.
  3. Verify the Delete Customer Records column in the configuration activity reference file.
  4. If deletion is not supported, change the entry description or stop using the wrong entry and create a correct one.
  5. If deletion is shown and supported but the button remains blocked, raise a case with SAP under the configuration activity component.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: NO_Broker Invoice Balance Due Not Visible After Payme...
entry.content:
Good day @vishnu-1996 

Thank you for your question.

That behaviour you described depends on whether the payment was posted as a partial payment or residual item.

With partial payment, the invoice and the payment remain as open items. The invoice is not reduced to the remaining balance in the line-item report. Therefore, the report may show the original invoice and the payment separately, rather than showing one updated invoice balance due on the invoice now.

With residual item processing, the original invoice is cleared, and anew open item is created for the remaining balance. This is the better standard option if the business requirement is to display the outstanding balance as a separate open receivable or payable item.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/279bad94d6414e05aab0cacf42eb3803.html?locale=en-US

3399348 - Partial payment and residual payment in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

2630879 - System does not take into partial payments related to the invoice in manual payment - SAP for Me

 

Here are the recommended next steps:

  1. Check how the payment was posted. Whether it was a partial payment or residual item.
  2. If your users expect only the remaining balance to remain open, use residual item processing.
  3. If partial payment is used, then make sure you analyse invoice and payment together using the customer or supplier line-item apps, invoice reference, assignment, payment reference, and account balance apps.
  4. If the item has clearing document but still appears open, check KBA 3169351 before you raise a case with SAP.
  5. You can test customer payments with Clear Incoming Payments.                                     
  6. For supplier payments, test with Clear Outgoing Payments.                                                                          
  7. Validate the result in Manage Customer/Supplier Line Items                                                                    

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/37e57e54279c2457e10000000a44176d.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/86248754a0cd9d62e10000000a445394.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

I would like to understand the potential impact of this data transfer on the Compensation module, specifically:

  • How does the system treat historical compensation data when an inactive employee is reactivated?
  • Will the previous compensation records (e.g., salary, pay components, merit increases) automatically flow into the current compensation cycle?

Any insights, best practices, or relevant documentation would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
SAP SuccessFactors Cybersecurity Übersicht
entry.content:
SAP SuccessFactors Cybersecurity

SAP SuccessFactors gehört als Human Capital Management (HCM) Suite zu den sensibelsten Cloud-Anwendungen im SAP-Portfolio: SuccessFactors verarbeitet personenbezogene Mitarbeiterdaten, darunter Gehaltsabrechnungen, Leistungsbeurteilungen und Bewerbungsunterlagen. Datenkategorien, die unter den regulatorischen Schutz der Datenschutz-Grundverordnung (DSGVO) fallen.

Dieser Artikel möchte Ihnen einen Einblick in die Cybersecurity-Maßnahmen rund um SAP SuccessFactors geben. Folgende Themen greifen wir auf:

  • SAP SuccessFactors Cloud Security Konzept
  • Datensicherheit – Verschlüsselung, Trennung, Löschung von Daten
  • Anwendungssicherheit – Zugriffe, Protokolle, SuccessFactors Mobile App
  • Betriebssicherheit – Monitoring, Resilienz, Patch-Mgmnt, Business Continuity
  • Vertragliche Grundlage
  • Transparenz und Compliance – Zertifikate  und Prüfberichte
  • Übergreifendes Cybersecurity Konzept für SAP Cloud Services

Cloud Security Konzept SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors ist eine Software as a Service (SaaS) Lösung. In diesem Cloud Modell liegt eine erhebliche Sicherheitsverantwortung beim Cloud Service Provider.

SAP verantwortet die Infrastruktur, einschließlich Bereitstellung der kundenspezifischen Instanzen. Dies umfasst auch die folgenden Leistungsmerkmale und Aufgaben:

  • Sichere Softwareentwicklung
  • Betriebsführung
  • Sicherung der Plattformarchitektur
  • 24x7 Security Monitoring & Logging
  • Patch- und Bedrohungsmanagement
  • Pentests und Schwachstellentests
  • Resilienz und Hochverfügbarkeit
  • Business Continuity Management
  • Managed Backup and Restore
  • Incident and Change Management
  • 24x7 Kunden Support
  • Prüfung und Einbindung von Unterauftragsverarbeitern
  • Security-Zertifikate und Prüfberichte
  • Dokumentierte Compliance der Cloud-Services

Bei SAP SuccessFactors verantwortet der Kunde die Konfiguration sowie die Benutzer- und Datenpflege in einer Instanz. Das umfasst auch die folgenden Aufgaben:

  • Einrichtung Benutzer
  • Einrichtung Identity Management
  • Definition und Zuweisung von Benutzerrollen und Benutzergruppen
  • Definition der Berechtigungen
  • Konfiguration der Geschäftsprozesse
  • Einrichtung und Aktivierung von Integrationen
  • Berücksichtigung von Compliance-Anforderungen
  • Security Audit Logging Verantwortung in der Instanz
  • Pflege der Daten
  • Fristgerechte Löschung von Daten

SAP klassifiziert alle Kundendaten in SAP SuccessFactors als vertraulich und wendet die im Datenverarbeitungsvertrag (SAP DPA) definierten Sicherheitsmaßnahmen (TOMs) an.

SAP SuccessFactors wird im Rahmen eines regionalen Bereitstellungsmodell angeboten. Die Rechenzentren für unsere europäischen Kunden befinden sich in der Europäischen Union, dem EWR oder der Schweiz. Dabei greift SAP auf eine Kombination aus eigenen Rechenzentren und Hyperscaler Infrastrukturen zurück.

Cloud Datensicherheit

Der Schutzbedarf der Daten in SAP SuccessFactors ist hoch. Personenbezogene Daten, darunter möglicherweise besondere Kategorien personenbezogener Daten gemäß Artikel 9, DSGVO, treten im Kontext mit unternehmensbezogenen Daten auf, die vertraulich und geschäftskritisch sein können, aber auch Konfigurationsdaten, die als sicherheitsrelevant eingestuft werden müssen, da Fehlkonfigurationen die Verarbeitung gefährden können.

Zum Schutz dieser Daten werden die folgenden Maßnahmen bereitgestellt.

Verschlüsslung gespeicherter Daten

SAP stellt Data-at-Rest-Verschlüsslung im Standard bereit. Wenn Daten abgelegt werden, dann werden sie auch verschlüsselt. Die Verschlüsselung erfolgt mittels AES 256 Standard. Für die Verwaltung der Schlüssel ist SAP verantwortlich.

Darüber hinaus bietet SAP für SAP SuccessFactors auch kundeneigene Verschlüsselung an d.h. Unternehmen und Organisationen, die Kontrolle über ihre kryptografischen Schlüssel benötigen, können den (aufpreispflichtigen) SAP Key Management Service (SAP KMS) nutzen. Dieser unterstützt drei Betriebsszenarien:

  • Customer Specific Encryption Keys (CSEK)
  • Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)
  • Hold Your Own Key (HYOK)

SAP KMS bietet FIPS-140-2-Konformität im Level 3, Funktionstrennung (Segregation of Duties), Master Key Management und die Protokollierung aller Schlüsselzugriffe.

Verschlüsslung übertragener Daten

Daten während der Übertragung (Data-in-Transit) werden mittels TLS 1.2 oder TLS 1.3. Protokoll und starken Chiffren verschlüsselt.

Beim Datenaustausch via OData API‘s wird zertifikatbasierte Authentifizierung unterstützt.

Dateiaustausche werden via SFTP geschützt. Der Schutz der Dateien kann durch eine Verschlüsselung auf Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) Basis noch ausgeweitet werden.

Datensicherung

SAP SuccessFactors stellt standardmäßig für alle Kundendatenbanksicherungen folgendes bereit:

  • Tägliche inkrementelle Sicherungen.
  • Wöchentliche vollständige Datenbanksicherungen.
  • Alle Sicherungen werden nach dem AES-256-Bit-Standard verschlüsselt.
  • Sicherungen werden sowohl im Produktivrechenzentrum als auch im Backuprechenzentrum des Kunden gespeichert.
  • Sicherungen werden 30 Tage lang gespeichert.
  • Produktionskunden können eine Wiederherstellung aus einer Sicherung über den Kundensupport anfordern.

SAP verfügt über einen formalen Systemsicherungsprozess und -zeitplan für die SAP-Cloud-Lösungen, der hardwareunabhängige Wiederherstellungs- und Wiederherstellungsfunktionen umfasst. Es sind geeignete Prozesse und automatisierte Tools vorhanden, um die Integrität der Sicherung zu validieren, und Sicherungsprotokolle werden täglich überprüft, um Sicherungsfehler zu erkennen und zu korrigieren.

Sichere Datentrennung

SuccessFactors läuft als Mehr-Mandanten-Anwendung mit logischer Datentrennung auf Datenbankebene. Die logische Trennung von kundenspezifischen Daten wird mittels HANA Datenbankschemen umgesetzt. Ein Kunde kann nur auf die Daten seines Unternehmens zugreifen.

Datenlöschung

Die Einhaltung von Löschfristen wird durch das Datenaufbewahrungsmanagement in SAP SuccessFactors gesteuert. Hier können maßgeschneiderte Löschkonzepte abgebildet werden, orientiert an relevanten Zeitpunkten und abgestimmt auf die relevante Datenkategorie z.B. Daten der Bewerbenden sowie auf das betroffene Land, in dem die Löschregel zum Einsatz kommt.

Basierend auf diesen Regeln, können wiederkehrende Löschläufe durch den Kunden eingeplant werden. Sobald die erfolgreiche Prüfung der Ergebnisse vorliegt, kann eine Freigabe erfolgen und die Löschung durchgeführt werden.

Nach Vertragsende werden SAP SuccessFactors Daten durch SAP gemäß den vereinbarten Prozessen und den geltenden Datenschutzvorgaben sicher gelöscht. Der Datenlöschungsprozess wird automatisch ausgelöst, sobald ein Vertrag endet oder gekündigt wird. Die vollständige Löschung der Daten erfolgt nach Validierung und Ablauf von Kulanzfristen. Die sichere Entfernung und Vernichtung von Datenträgern ist Bestandteil der SAP-Standards und wird durch Audits überprüft.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Cloud Anwendungssicherheit

Identitätsmanagement

SAP SuccessFactors wird mit dem SAP Identity Management Services bereitgestellt. Diese bieten Authentifizierung über Industriestandards wie SAML 2.0 und unterstützen Single Sign-On sowie die Delegation an unternehmenseigene Identity Provider wie Azure Active Directory.

Benutzeridentitäten zwischen Quell- und Zielsystemen können auf Basis des SCIM-Standards synchronisiert werden.

SAP SuccessFactors unterstützt zudem die  zertifikatsbasierte Authentifizierung für die sichere API-Kommunikation.

Zugriffssteuerung

Die Zugriffssteuerung von SAP SuccessFactors basiert auf rollenbasierten Berechtigungen, einem granularen, rollenbasierten Zugriffssteuerungssystem. Zugriffsrechte sind granular, werden auf Feld-, Datensatz- und Funktionsebene definiert und werden Benutzern, Rollen oder Gruppen zugewiesen.

Sichere Softwareentwicklung

SAP entwickelt SAP SuccessFactors nach dem Secure Software Development and Operations Lifecycle, der risikobasierte Sicherheitsanforderungen in jede Entwicklungsphase integriert.

Security Gates, Threat Modeling  sowie Statische (SAST) und dynamische (DAST) Sicherheitsanalysen sind nur einige Elemente dieses umfassenden Vorgehens bei der Entwicklung.

Audit Trails und Protokolle

SAP SuccessFactors bietet Kunden Zugang zu Application Security Audit Logs auf Anwendungsebene, darunter befinden sich u.a. der General Audit Bericht, die Änderungsverfolgung (Change Audit), Kontrollierte Einsicht (Read Access) für sensible Daten. Sicherheitsprotokolle auf Ebene der SAP SuccessFactors Instanz können in ein kundeneigenes SIEM-System überführt werden.

Darüber hinaus existieren spezialisierte Audit-Funktionen wie Audit Trails im Recruiting und in Performance & Goals, wesentlich für die Nachvollziehbarkeit von Personalentscheidungen.

SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App

Die SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App wird für iOS und Android Betriebssysteme bereitgestellt. Die Installation ist auf unternehmenseigenen und privaten Endgeräten möglich.

Die Authentifizierung bei Zugriffen durch die Mobile App erfolgt mittels OAuth Token. Die App kann mittels Passwortes oder gerätespezifischer Möglichkeiten (Fingerabdruck, Gesichtsscan) vor unberechtigtem Zugriff geschützt werden.

Unabhängig vom Endgerät sind die Berechtigungen der Nutzenden auf der SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App: Basis sind die im System hinterlegten Berechtigungen.

Alle Datenübertragungen sind verschlüsselt, wenn es zu einer Ablage von Daten auf der App kommt, so sind auch diese verschlüsselt.

Die SAP SuccessFactors Applikation für Mobilgeräte unterstützt die marktführenden Mobile Device Management (MDM) Systeme.

Cloud Betriebssicherheit

Netzwerksicherheit

SAP hat eine mehrstufige Netzwerk-Architektur implementiert, die Zero-Trust-Prinzipien folgt und eine Strategie der "Tiefenverteidigung“ nutzt. Der Netzwerkverkehr wird kontinuierlich durch ein Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS) überwacht und protokolliert.

SAP unterhält intern eine strikte Zugriffssteuerungsrichtlinie, die einen rollenbasierten Zugriff auf alle Ressourcen (Anwendungen, Betriebssysteme, Netzwerkgeräte usw.) fordert sowie eine eindeutige ID für alle Personen. Berechtigungen werden nach den Segregation-of-Duties und Need-to-Know Prinzipien vergeben.

Die Sicherheit des SAP-Netzwerks einschließlich der SAP-Endbenutzerausrüstung wird zusätzlich durch Lösungen wie Netzwerkzugangssteuerung, Netzwerkfilterung, starke Authentifizierung, Internet-Inhaltsfilterung und Antiviren-Scanner gewährleistet.

Monitoring

SAP betreibt ein 24/7 Security Monitoring für die SAP SuccessFactors-Plattform. Sicherheitsereignisse werden zentral in einem SIEM System korreliert; bei verdächtigen oder bösartigen Aktivitäten werden automatische Alerts und Security Incident Tickets erzeugt.

Die Nachverfolgung von Tickets durch die Mitarbeitenden im Operations-Team folgt dem SAP Security Incident Management Prozess, die Tätigkeiten werden dokumentiert und sind revisionsfähig.

Logging

Alle relevanten technischen Infrastruktur-Komponenten die den Betrieb des SAP SuccessFactors Service unterstützen, sind an ein SIEM System angeschlossen. Die dort protokollierten Daten umfassen auch alle Login-Versuche um ggf. forensische Untersuchungen zu unterstützen.

Resiliente Plattformarchitektur

SAP stellt für SAP SuccessFactors eine Multi-Availability-Zone-Architektur bereit. Diese wird jeweils ergänzt durch ein Sekundär-Rechenzentrum um eine zeitnahe Widerherstellung des Services bei einem Rechenzentrumsausfall zu gewährleisten.

Business Continuity Management

SAP SuccessFactors ist nach ISO 22301 zertifiziert (Business Continuity Management System).

SAP SuccessFactors sichert die Daten seiner Auftraggeber kontinuierlich und hält diese über einen Zeitraum von 30 Tagen zur Verfügung. Eine Kopie der Datensicherung befindet sich jederzeit in dem vorgesehenen  BackUp Rechenzentrum.

Es werden jährliche Disaster Recovery Tests durchgeführt, der zu Grunde liegende Disaster Recovery Plan wird regelmäßig geprüft und aktualisiert.

Vulnerability Management und Penetration Testing

SAP führt regelmäßige Schwachstellenanalysen (Vulnerability Scans) und Penetrationstests durch und stellt diese seinen Kunden auf Nachfrage zur Verfügung. Kunden können, in Abstimmung mit SAP, eigene Penetrationstests durchführen.

Patch- und Change-Management

SAP verwaltet das Patching der SAP SuccessFactors-Plattform, einschließlich Sicherheits-Patches für Betriebssysteme, Datenbank, Middleware und Anwendungsschicht.

Bei der Priorisierung von Schwachstellen orientiert sich SAP am Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).

Vertragliche Grundlage

Die SAP SuccessFactors Vertragsgrundlage umfasst folgende Dokumente:

Vertragsbestandteil

Inhalt

Order Form

Kunde & SAP (Kontaktdaten, Leistungsumfang, Beginn & Ende…)

AGB’s

Nutzungsrechte, SAP Verantwortlichkeiten und Pflichten, Vergütung, Gewährleistung, Vertraulichkeit, Kündigung…

Ergänzende Bedingungen

Nutzungsmetriken, zusätzl. Bedingungen, Disaster Recovery…

Support Schedule

Kontaktkanäle, Sprachen, Prioritäten und Reaktionszeiten

Service Level Agreement

Systemverfügbarkeit, Wartungsfenster, Gutschriften

Auftragsverarbeitungs-vertrag

Sicherheit Verarbeitung, Leistungspflichten SAP, Zertifizierung und Audits, Unterauftragsverarbeiter…

Sicherheitsmaßnahmen (TOM´s)

Governance der Informationssicherheit, Sicherheits-zertifizierungen und -bescheinigungen, Organisatorische, physische und technische Sicherheitsmaßnahmen, Resilienz, Umgang mit Sicherheitsvorfällen…

Transparenz und Compliance

SAP SuccessFactors unterliegt regelmäßigen, unabhängigen Audits und hält eine Vielzahl von anerkannten Zertifikaten und Prüfberichte bereit. Darunter befinden sich:

Zertifikat, Prüfbericht

Inhalt

ISO 27001

Informationssicherheits-Managementsystem

ISO 27017

Schutz personenbezogener Daten in der Cloud

ISO 27018

Sicherheitsmaßnahmen für Cloud‑Dienste

ISO 22301

Business Continuity Managementsystem

BS 10012

Datenschutz-Managementsystem

EU Cloud Code of Conduct

Genehmigte Verhaltensregeln, Artikel 41/42 DSGVO

ISAE 3000 BSI C5 Type II

Prüfbericht Sicherheitsmaßnahmen gem. BSI

ISAE 3402 SOC 1 Type II

Prüfbericht interne Kontrollen

ISAE 3000 SOC 2 Type II

Prüfbericht Sicherheitsmaßnahmen

Aktuelle Zertifikate und Prüfberichte können im SAP Trust Center eingesehen oder beantragt werden: Alle Zertifikate Auditberichte SF

Übergreifendes Cybersecurity Konzept für SAP Cloud Services

Alle vorgestellten Sicherheitsmaßnahmen betten sich in die übergeordneten, konzernweiten Maßnahmen der SAP ein, beschrieben durch das SAP Security Policy Framework und durchgesetzt mit dem NIST Cyber Security Framework.

SAP Security Policy Framework

Die SAP Global Security Policy ist das übergeordnete Dokument, das die Management-Intention, Erwartungen und strategische Ausrichtung festlegt. Es definiert die strategischen Ziele und Vorgaben für ein höchstmögliches Sicherheitsniveau bei der Bereitstellung von SAP Cloud Services.

Konkret dient es als Leitfaden, der mit anerkannten Standards, klaren Sicherheitsregeln und Best Practices die Grundlage für Planung, Entwicklung, Konfiguration und Betrieb von SAP Cloud Lösungen darstellt.

Das Ziel: Einen einheitlichen, hohen Sicherheitsstandard über alle SAP Cloud Services zu gewährleisten!

Secure Data, Applications, and Data Centers | SAP Security

Einsatz des NIST Cyber Security Frameworks

SAP hat erfolgreich das NIST Cyber Security Framework (NIST CSF) implementiert und den Tier 3 Reifegrad erreicht.

In einer sich kontinuierlich wandelnden Bedrohungslandschaft bietet das NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) eine effektive und strukturierte Grundlage, um Sicherheits-Maßnahmen flexibel anzupassen und kontinuierlich weiterzuentwickeln. Es unterstützt uns dabei, neue Risiken systematisch zu identifizieren, zu bewerten und geeignete Schutzmaßnahmen gezielt an sich verändernde Anforderungen und Bedrohungen auszurichten.

SAP Security Policy Framework und NIST Cyber Security Framework ergänzen sich, stellen in ihrer Verbindung sicher, das alle Sicherheits-Vorgaben im Rahmen eines aktiven Risiko-Managements kontinuierlich geprüft und gegebenenfalls angepasst werden.

Das Erreichen von NIST CSF Tier 3 soll nicht nur das Vertrauen in unsere Produkte und Dienstleistungen stärken, sondern auch unser Engagement, Kunden zu schützen sowie Prozesse und Daten dauerhaft wirksam abzusichern.

How SAP is safeguarding its customers: Implementing the NIST Cybersecurity Framework achieving Tier 3 alignment

Fazit

SAP SuccessFactors steht aufgrund der Sensitivität von HR-Daten im Brennpunkt regulatorischer und sicherheitstechnischer Anforderungen.

SAP verfolgt einen durchgängigen Sicherheitsansatz, vom Shared-Responsibility-Modell mit klarer Verantwortungstrennung zwischen SAP und Kunde, über Datenverschlüsselung, granulare rollenbasierte Berechtigungen, nativ integrierte SAP Identity Management Services, bis hin zu 24x7-Security-Monitoring, Cyber Threat Intelligence und umfassender Audit- und Compliance-Berichte.

Die Wirksamkeit dieses Sicherheitsmodells hängt jedoch auch von der aktiven Mitwirkung des Kunden ab -- insbesondere bei der Konfiguration von Berechtigungen, beim Identity Management, Datenschutzeinstellungen und der regelmäßigen Überprüfung von Security Audit Logs.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Erfahren Sie mehr zu Sicherheit mit SAP für Daten, Anwendungen und Rechenzentren

entry.content:
Hi @xavierlegarrec , thank you for your reply.

In the meanwhile, I was able to find the root cause by comparing the last working version of the template with the first failing version. It turned out to be an entry level field that was mapped to a child custom MDF object. Not sure why this is an issue, nor why it would affect bonus calculation, since it's a custom entry level field, but removing that mapping fixed it. Leaving it here in case it might help others.

Thank you and best regards,

Diogo

entry.title: SAP EWM–PP Integration: PMR-Based Staging, Consumption, and Finished Goods Receipt
entry.content:
Abstract & Introduction:

In modern manufacturing environments, seamless integration between production planning and warehouse execution is essential for ensuring efficient material flow, inventory accuracy, and real-time operational visibility. SAP S/4HANA provides this capability through the integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP), enabling synchronized execution of production supply and warehouse processes.

This article presents an end-to-end overview of SAP EWM and PP integration, covering both system configuration and business process execution. The paper explains key integration components such as production supply areas (PSAs), production material requests (PMRs), staging methods, warehouse task creation, material consumption, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt processing. It also highlights the interaction between production orders and warehouse activities, demonstrating how materials are staged to production lines and how finished products are received and managed within EWM.

Objective is to provide both functional consultants and business users with a practical understanding of how SAP EWM and PP collaborate to support efficient and integrated manufacturing operations.

Production supply area(PSA):
A production supply area is the location where components are staged and made available for production consumption. Its purpose is to Supplies components to production and Acts as the destination for EWM staging.

Control Cycle:
A control cycle defines how a specific material is supplied from the warehouse to a specific PSA and controls the staging process for production.

Material + PSA + Plant -> Staging Method

SAP PP/EWM, Control Cycles are classified based on the staging method used to supply components to the PSA.

1. Pick Parts Staging: Components are staged for a specific production order and picked exactly according to the order requirement.
2. Release Order Parts Staging: Components are staged automatically when the production order is released.
3. Crate Parts Staging: Materials are replenished in the PSA based on minimum and maximum quantities instead of production order demand.

In SAP EWM Advanced Production Integration (API), the control cycle can be configured with EWM Staging. In this case, EWM controls the staging process through PMR (Production Material Request).

  1. Single-Order Staging: Materials are staged specifically for an individual production order.
  2. Cross-Order Staging: Materials are staged collectively for multiple production orders instead of one specific order.

Under this document, I am trying to make a scenario for a finish material # 1001590 and having three components.

#1 1001575 – EWM staging (Single order release)

#2 1001596 – Replenishment of Crate parts

#3 1001597 – Manual staging

Customization:

SAP S/4HANA

#1 Maintain Storage location(SLOC)

This is an organizational unit within a plant where materials are physically stored and inventory is managed. it belongs to exactly one plant and represents a physical or logical storage area. This is the lowest inventory management level in ERP/MM where stock quantities are maintained.

SPRO->Enterprise structure->Definition->Materials Management->Maintain Storage location

Created a SLOC called “AFS”, which is used in this document.

#2 Assign Warehouse number to Plant/Storage location

This configuration tells, whenever stock is posted to this Plant + Storage Location combination, the stock is physically managed in this EWM Warehouse Number.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->Map storage locations from ERP system to EWM

 

#3 Define stock types

Stock Type defines the inventory status of a product in EWM. It controls what operations can be performed on the stock and how it is represented between ERP and EWM.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Receipt Process->Configure availability group for put away->Configure stock type

 

#4 Storage type for Production supply area

In SAP EWM, a Storage Type for Production Supply Area (PSA) is the EWM storage type that represents the physical area where components are staged for production consumption. I am not showing other storage types but only showing PSA.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Master data->Define storage type

#5 Warehouse process type:

A Warehouse Process Type (WPT) controls how warehouse activities are executed in EWM. It determines the behavior of warehouse tasks such as putaway, picking, staging, internal movements, and production supply.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Define warehouse process type

WPT # 2010 is created for stock removal, especially for Staging process.

WPT # 3100 is created for replenishment, especially for crate parts.

#6 Determine warehouse process type:

This step is used to identify the warehouse process type depending upon the document type and Item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Determine warehouse process type

#7 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type:

This step link production order type to warehouse and document type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type

#8 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Item type:

This step link component type with order type, warehouse, document type and item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM item type

#9 Define Document types for production:

This is one of important step which assign action profile and other details to document type and category.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define document types for production

#10 Define item types for production:

This configuration step in SAP EWM that specifies the different item categories used in production integration documents, such as Production Material Requests (PMRs) and production supply processes. The item type controls how materials are processed, staged, consumed, and monitored during production activities

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define item types for production

#11 Allowed Item types for production

This setting in SAP EWM that specifies which production item types can be used within a particular production process or document. It ensures that only valid item categories are processed for staging, consumption, and goods receipt activities.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Allowed item types for production

#12 Customization delivery:

SCM Extended warehouse management->Extended Warehouse Management->Cross process Settings ->Delivery warehouse-request->Check Customising

Document type “OPC” is having below configuration details.

#13 Assign Storage type to search sequence:

Assign Storage Type to Search Sequence is a configuration step in SAP EWM that determines the order in which the system searches storage types when locating stock for warehouse activities such as picking, staging, or replenishment.

Storage type “0011” wherein we have a component stock.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Specify storage type search sequence

#14 Storage type search sequence:

This configuration in SAP EWM that defines the order in which the system searches storage types to find stock for warehouse processes such as picking, staging, replenishment, and internal movements. EWM checks each storage type in the defined sequence until sufficient stock is found.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Determine Specify storage type search sequence for stock removal

#15 Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types:

Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types is a configuration setting in SAP EWM that enables automatic replenishment of stock within a storage type when inventory falls below a predefined threshold. This ensures that sufficient inventory is always available to support warehouse operations and production processes without manual intervention.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Internal warehouse processes->Replenishment control->Activate replenishment strategies in storage type

Master data:

#1 Map the EWM PSA to the ERP PSA(/SCWM/PSAMAP):

This step that links the Production Supply Area (PSA) defined in SAP EWM with the corresponding PSA in ERP/PP. This mapping enables seamless integration between production and warehouse processes, allowing EWM to stage components to the correct production area based on Production Material Requests (PMRs). It ensures that materials are delivered to the appropriate location for production consumption.

#2 Work Center (CR01):

A Work Center in SAP PP represents the physical location, machine, production line, or group of resources where manufacturing operations are performed. It is used in routings and production orders to define where an operation will be executed and to provide scheduling, capacity planning, and costing information. In SAP EWM production integration, the work center can also be linked to a Production Supply Area (PSA) to support component staging and consumption processes.

#3 Created BOM(CS01):

A Bill of Material (BOM) is a structured list of all raw materials, components, semi-finished products, and quantities required to manufacture a finished product. It defines what materials are needed for production and in what quantity. During production order creation, SAP uses the BOM to determine component requirements, generate reservations, and create Production Material Requests (PMRs) for staging in SAP EWM.

Material # 1001590 is finish product and having three components (1001575, 1001596, 1001597) and made below BOM.

#4 Component stock availability(/SCWM/MON):

Mentioned component stocks are available for staging and consumptions.

#5 Creation of Routing (CA01):

A Routing is a master data object in SAP PP that defines the sequence of operations required to manufacture a product. It specifies the work center, operation steps, processing times, and production activities needed to complete the manufacturing process. During production order creation, SAP uses the routing for scheduling, capacity planning, and determining where components staged from SAP EWM will be consumed.

Created routing for finish product # 1001590.

#6 Control Cycle (LPK1):

A Control Cycle is a master data object in SAP PP that defines how materials are supplied from the warehouse to a Production Supply Area (PSA) for manufacturing. For Material # 1001575, a control cycle was created to define how the material is supplied from the warehouse to the production area. The EWM Staging Indicator (5) specifies that the material will be supplied using Production Material Request (PMR)-based staging in SAP EWM. During production order creation, EWM automatically generates a PMR and stages the required components to the designated Production Supply Area (PSA) based on the control cycle settings.

 

 

Below control Cycle for material #1001596, defines how a material is supplied from the warehouse to the Production Supply Area (PSA). It links the material, work center/PSA, warehouse, and staging method used for production supply. Staging Indicator = 2 (Crate Part Staging). Crate Part Staging means EWM maintains a fixed number of containers (crates) in the PSA. The system monitors the quantity available in the PSA and automatically replenishes the crates when stock falls below the required level.

 

Below control cycle for material # 1001597, with Manual Staging, SAP EWM does not automatically create replenishment requests or stage materials to the PSA. The warehouse user must manually trigger the staging process and move the required quantity from the warehouse to the PSA bin.

 

#7 Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM(/SCWM/PSASTAGE):

Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM is a step that defines the specific PSA storage bin where a particular product will be staged for production. It links the product (and, if applicable, the entitled party) to a designated PSA bin, ensuring that SAP EWM delivers components to the correct production supply location.

#8 Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

Product master is created with stock removal control as “PICK”

End to End Transactions:

#1 Production order (CO01):

Below production order is created for finish material # 1001590. And all three components are assign to operation # 10.

#2 Staging for Single order release parts (CO02):

Control cycle for material # 1001575 was created as EWM staging and single order release.

#3 PMR display(/SCWM/MON):

After material staging step, below PMR was created for material # 1001575.

#4 Creation of staging task(/SCWM/STAGE):

Staging task was created for material # 1001575, because this material is linked with EWM staging.

 

#5 Warehouse task display(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task # 100068 created for material # 1001575 to move the stock(staging) from 0011(normal storage type) to 1005(PSA storage type) + specific bin # PSA1-1005-BIN1

#6 Replenishment for the crate part(/SCWM/REPL):

This replenishment task is created for material # 1001596, as control cycle for this material is created as Crate part staging.

Warehouse Task # 100069(/SCWM/MON):

#7 Manual staging(/SCWM/ADPROD):

Manual staging can be done by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/ADPROD(GUI). As in this example, I am using /SCWM/ADPROD.

 

#8 Summarized Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Below three warehouse tasks are created for all three components.

#9 Confirm Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task can be confirmed by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/MON(GUI), however I am confirming using /SCWM/MON.

#10 Stock status in PSA bins (/SCWM/MON):

After warehouse tasks confirmation, stock is correctly populated in PSA bins.

#11 Consumption of components against Production order (/SCWM/RFUI):

First component # 1001575 was consumed using manufacturing order # 1001345.

#12 Consumption of components using CO15:

Remaining two components (#1001596, 1001597) are consumed using production order confirmation.

 

#13 Outbound delivery order((/SCWM/PRDO)

After production order confirmation, below outbound delivery order was created

#14 Outbound delivery(/SCWM/PRDO):

After post goods issue, below outbound delivery was created.

#15 Goods receipt of Finish material:

After Production order confirmation ERP Inbound delivery # 180109341 was created automatically and as a next step Inbound delivery # 325 was created in EWM automatically.

#16 Warehouse task(/SCWM/MON):

Below warehouse task was confirmed and now stock is available.

Conclusion:

The integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP) in SAP S/4HANA provides a robust framework for synchronizing warehouse and manufacturing operations. By connecting production orders with warehouse execution processes, organizations can ensure timely material availability, improve inventory accuracy, and enhance overall production efficiency.

Through key integration components such as Production Material Requests (PMRs), Production Supply Areas (PSAs), material staging, warehouse task processing, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt management, SAP enables a seamless flow of information and materials across the manufacturing environment. This integration eliminates manual interventions, increases process visibility, and supports real-time inventory updates throughout the production lifecycle.

By implementing the configuration and process steps described in this article, businesses can establish a tightly integrated production supply chain that supports efficient manufacturing execution, accurate inventory management, and enhanced warehouse productivity within SAP S/4HANA.

 Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavors like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: Processing Serial Numbers in Handling Units within an RF Environment in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Serial number tracking in Handling Units (HUs) is a common practice and is highly relevant in industries like electronics, pharmaceuticals, and medical devices where the item is stored and traced with its unique identification from receipt to shipment. In this blog, I will demonstrate two practical approaches to capture serial numbers during the inbound process: one via the /SCWM/PRDI packing screen, and the other entirely in the RF environment. Both methods give the same result, choosing between them ultimately comes down to how your warehouse processes are designed.

For this process, I have chosen a material with serialization procedure C (Serial Number at stock level) to ensure traceability at individual quant.

Step 1:  Navigate to transaction ME21N to Create a purchase order for the serialized material.

Step 2: Navigate to transaction VL31N and enter your purchase order to create the Inbound Delivery and save. The Inbound delivery is distributed to EWM.

Method 1: Packing the material in the /SCWM/PRDI screen

Step 3: Pack material in HU

  • Go to /SCWM/PRDI transaction. Enter delivery number and execute.
  • Select Inbound Delivery->Follow-on Functions ->Pack
  • Pack the product into the HU.
  • Double click on the material and navigate to Serial Nos tab to enter the serial numbers and then save.

Step 4: Create the HU Warehouse task.

  • Select Inbound Delivery -> Follow-on Functions - > Warehouse task.
  • Select Handling Units tab
  • Upon clicking on Create+Save the Warehouse Order is created for the HU.

Step 5: Confirm the Warehouse task in RF

  • Navigate to transaction /SCWM/RFUI. Go to Inbound Process- > Putaway - > Putaway by WO. Enter the Warehouse Order.
  • Scan the source bin and source HU

  • Scan the destination bin and destination HU.

  • The warehouse task is confirmed, and the Goods Receipt (GR) is posted automatically.

Method 2:  Updating serial numbers directly in the RF Environment

Step 3:  Navigate to RF Receiving

  • Go to transaction /SCWM/RFUI.
  • Select Inbound Process- > Receiving of Handling Units - > Receive HU by Delivery.
  • Enter the Delivery Number and press Enter twice.

Step 4: Select New HU to create a new Handling unit.

Step 5: Enter the product and quantity and click Next.

Step 6: Enter the appropriate Packaging material and click on Next.

Step 7: Select SerNo button to enter the Serial number for the product. Update the serial number and click on save

Step 8: After saving the serial numbers and navigate back to the main screen. Click on F3 (Create) and then F4 (Confirm) the Warehouse task. Upon confirming you will be directed to the next screen to confirm the bin and HU data.

Step 9: Verify the Destination Bin and Destination HU and click on ente

Step 10: Once the Warehouse task is confirmed, we can see that the GR is posted automatically which can be verified in ERP.

Conclusion:

Both the approaches produce the same outcome i.e., receiving serial numbers in Handling Units in the RF environment. Choose Method 1 if packing is performed at a dedicated packing station where RF intervention is not required. Choose Method 2 if packing and goods receipt are simultaneously performed with an RF device on the warehouse floor.  Ultimately, it comes down to your business process followed in your warehouse.

I hope this blog gave some meaningful insights. Feel free to share your thoughts in the comments below.

entry.title: Requirement: Automated Detection of Partition Failures in Replication Flow
entry.content:
Currently, when a partition fails, the object remains in a Retrying state and does not clearly indicate a partition-level failure at the object level.

Our requirement is to automatically identify partition failures and receive notifications/alerts whenever a partition fails. At present, the team is manually monitoring each object and checking partition statuses individually, which is very time-consuming and operationally inefficient.

Could you please confirm:

  1. Whether there is any standard mechanism to notify users when a partition fails.
  2. Whether partition-level failures can be captured through APIs, monitoring tools, system tables, or logs.
  3. Whether SAP recommends any approach to automate the detection and alerting of partition failures instead of manually checking each object.

Any guidance or best practices for implementing such monitoring would be greatly appreciated.

Is there an option to prioritize truck over air when both transport modes are having the same delivery time?

 

Thank you,

 

Martin

entry.title: Re: HU Screen Not Visible During Goods Receipt with Produ...
entry.content:
Good day @kkumara 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshot and description, you are trying to receive goods for production order into an EWM manage storage location.

The first thing to clarify is that simply extending the material to the warehouse storage location is not enough to make the HU screen appear. For production order goods receipt, the HU and Warehouse Management fields depend on the activated warehouse production integration process, the receiving storage location, synchronous goods movement settings and whether the production GR is processed with or without an EWM inbound delivery.

Note that goods receipt can be posted manually, for example with Post Goods Movement app, automatically with Confirm Production Order/Confirm Production Order Operation. The receiving storage location is taken from the production order header.

For EWM integration, Warehouse Production Integration with Synchronous Goods Receipt 5HO allow produced products to be posted directly into the warehouse without delivery documents and the process can include Pack Finished Material, Create HU Putaway Warehouse Task, and Confirm Putaway Warehouse Tasks.

So, in simple terms:

  • If the production GR is not relevant for the warehouse integration scenario, the HU or Warehouse Management section will not appear.
  • IF the storage location in the production order is not the EWM managed receiving storage location, the warehouse tab will not behave as expected.
  • If synchronous goods movement is not active or not correctly mapped for the storage location, the Warehouse Management tab my not be shown.
  • Material extension alone does not activate the HU screen.
  • Depending on your design, the process must be aligned with scope item 3DV Warehouse Production Integration or 5HO Warehouse Production Integration with Synchronous Goods Receipt

See KBA 3249204 which is very close to your situation, and it explains the system logic for determining the storage bin when posting goods receipt for production orders with or without inbound deliveries to an EWM warehouse. Also check KBA 3451929 for confirmation on HU handling unit being relevant for goods receipt without delivery in the Post Goods Movement app.

Basically, this means the following:

  • There is no separate material master field that simply “turns on” the HU screen for production GR.
  • First check whether your production order GR is being posted into an EWM managed storage location.
  • Then check whether the process is delivery based or synchronous goods movement based.
  • If it is delivery based, you normally check the warehouse information through the production inbound delivery process.
  • If it’s synchronous goods receipt, the Warehouse Management tab and HU related actions depend on the synchronous goods movement setup and warehouse mapping.

See KBA 3498212 for more details tab visibility and its dependencies on exact business process and movement scenario.

 

To summarise:

The HU screen is not controlled only by the fact that you extended the material to a warehouse storage location. For production order goods receipt, the system must treat the receipt as warehouse relevant. This depends on the production order receiving storage location, the activated warehouse production integration scope, the storage location to warehouse mapping, and whether you use synchronous goods movement or inbound delivery-based process.

As a next step, check the receiving storage location, confirm that it is mapped to the warehouse and confirm the warehouse production integration scope is active. Then test the same order in Post Goods Movement with movement type 101 

If the process creates or uses inbound delivery, check it in Change Inbound Deliveries-Production.   

If the setup is correct and the tab is still missing only for production order GR, then raise a case with SAP.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Batch number not generated for multipe co products on releasing process order
entry.content:
Faced issue on batch generation for multiple coproduct on releasing the process order. Later got the issue.

I have created new material with out coproduct check and added to BOM as component. Later released it. System not generated batch for co product.
Later added coproduct at material master and created new process order and released. Still not got the batch.

Then i got to know that the co product check is not updated in BOM as i created it with change number. Then I have added the co product check in BOM manually and then created new process order and released. Now its generating the Batch for co product. 


So the system checking the coproduct check at BOM level for generating the batch.
